description: Fix minor resource leaks and add missing cleanup in the Chrome extension's background service worker: prune the clearedConfirmationIds set, prune stale wsFramesByRequestId entries in network capture, and add a guard against concurrent startCapture calls for the same tab.

userStories.title: Prune clearedConfirmationIds to prevent unbounded growth
userStories.description: As a developer, I want the clearedConfirmationIds set in confirmation-badge.ts to be pruned after entries are no longer needed so that memory does not grow unboundedly during long browser sessions with many confirmation requests.
acceptanceCriteria: After a confirmation is resolved and both the side panel and background timeout paths have completed, the confirmation ID is removed from clearedConfirmationIds
clearedConfirmationIds does not grow beyond the number of currently-pending confirmations plus a small buffer for in-flight timeout races
Existing idempotency guarantee is preserved: calling clearConfirmationBadge twice with the same id still decrements the count only once
clearAllConfirmationBadges still clears the entire set on disconnect
userStories.notes:
File: platform/browser-extension/src/confirmation-badge.ts

The `clearedConfirmationIds` Set (line 18) tracks confirmation IDs that have already been cleared to prevent double-decrementing the badge count. The problem: when the side panel resolves a confirmation first (sp:confirmationResponse), the flow is:
1. `clearConfirmationBackgroundTimeout(id)` — deletes from confirmationTimeouts AND clearedConfirmationIds (line 129)
2. `clearConfirmationBadge(id)` — id is NOT in clearedConfirmationIds (just deleted), so it adds it back (line 109), decrements count
3. The background timeout was cancelled, so it never fires to delete the entry
4. The ID stays in clearedConfirmationIds permanently until the next disconnect

Fix approach: Add a cleanup step that removes the ID from clearedConfirmationIds after both paths have definitively settled. One clean approach: in `clearConfirmationBadge`, after adding to clearedConfirmationIds, schedule a microtask or short setTimeout to remove the ID once the concurrent timeout race window has passed. Alternatively, remove from clearedConfirmationIds when the corresponding confirmationTimeouts entry is absent (meaning no background timeout can race).

The simplest correct fix: in `clearConfirmationBadge`, after the idempotency check succeeds and the count is decremented, also delete the entry from `confirmationTimeouts` if present (it's already cleared), and then use a `queueMicrotask` or just inline the deletion of the clearedConfirmationIds entry when there's no corresponding confirmationTimeouts entry. Since the race window is only between the bg timeout callback and the side panel message, and both run on the same event loop, by the time the next macrotask runs, the race is settled.

Actually the simplest approach: in `clearConfirmationBadge`, after adding the id to `clearedConfirmationIds` and decrementing the count, check if `confirmationTimeouts.has(id)` is false — if so, the bg timeout either already fired or was cancelled, so the id is safe to prune immediately from clearedConfirmationIds. If it's true, leave it for the bg timeout callback to prune (which it already does at line 79).

userStories.title: Prune stale wsFramesByRequestId entries in network capture
userStories.description: As a developer, I want stale entries in the wsFramesByRequestId map to be pruned periodically so that long-running network captures on pages with many WebSocket connections do not leak memory.
acceptanceCriteria: The periodic prune interval in startCapture also prunes wsFramesByRequestId entries that are older than PENDING_REQUEST_TTL_MS
The inline prune in the Network.requestWillBeSent handler also prunes wsFramesByRequestId entries
WebSocket frame capture still works correctly for active connections
userStories.notes:
File: platform/browser-extension/src/network-capture.ts

The `wsFramesByRequestId` map (line 96 in CaptureState) maps WebSocket requestIds to their URLs. Entries are added on `Network.webSocketCreated` (line 309-311) and removed on `Network.webSocketClosed` (line 347-350). However, if a WebSocket connection is never cleanly closed (tab navigates away mid-connection, connection drops without close frame), the entry persists indefinitely.

The existing prune interval (lines 449-461) and inline prune in requestWillBeSent (lines 189-199) already prune `pendingRequests` and `requestIdToRequest` but NOT `wsFramesByRequestId`.

Fix: Add `wsFramesByRequestId` pruning to both the prune interval and the inline prune. Since wsFramesByRequestId entries don't have a timestamp, the simplest approach is to track creation time. Add a `wsCreatedAt` Map<string, number> to CaptureState that records when each WebSocket requestId was first seen. Then prune entries from both maps when the creation timestamp exceeds PENDING_REQUEST_TTL_MS. Alternatively, use a longer TTL (e.g., 5 minutes) since WebSocket connections are expected to be long-lived.

Note: The `getWsFrames` function (line 534-544) clears `wsFramesByRequestId` when `clear=true`, and `stopCapture` (line 470-478) clears it. The leak only affects long-running captures where WebSocket connections open and silently die.

userStories.title: Guard against concurrent startCapture calls for the same tab
userStories.description: As a developer, I want startCapture to handle concurrent calls for the same tab gracefully so that a race between two rapid enableNetworkCapture requests does not produce a confusing 'Failed to attach debugger' error.
acceptanceCriteria: If startCapture is called for a tab that has a pending (in-flight) startCapture, the second call waits for the first to complete and then returns without re-attaching
The existing check at line 415-417 (throws if capture already active) still works for non-racing calls
If the first startCapture fails, the second call does not incorrectly report the tab as capturing
userStories.notes:
File: platform/browser-extension/src/network-capture.ts

The `startCapture` function (line 408) checks `captures.has(tabId)` synchronously, then does async work (chrome.debugger.attach, sendCommand). The capture state is only stored in the `captures` map at line 463, AFTER all async operations complete. If two startCapture calls arrive for the same tabId before the first completes:
1. Both pass the `captures.has(tabId)` check (returns false)
2. First call: chrome.debugger.attach succeeds
3. Second call: chrome.debugger.attach throws 'Another debugger is already attached'
4. Second call throws to the caller with a confusing error

Fix: Add a `pendingCaptures` Set<number> that tracks tab IDs with in-flight startCapture calls. Check this set alongside `captures.has(tabId)`. Add to the set before the async work, remove in finally block. If the tab is in pendingCaptures, either throw a clear error ('Capture already starting for tab X') or return silently.

Alternative simpler approach: use a per-tab promise lock similar to the `withPluginLock` pattern in tab-state.ts, so concurrent calls are serialized and the second one sees the capture as already active.
